title: Preparing Sumi Ink
subtitle: Grinding an inkstick to working density on a suzuri stone
excerpt: Freshly ground sumi ink behaves differently from bottled ink: the particle size is finer, the surface tension lower, and the depth of tone is fully under your control. This technique covers water quantity, grinding motion, density testing, and inkstone care.
sourceNotes: General knowledge of the East-Asian ink-grinding tradition, as documented in pre-Meiji calligraphy primers.

glossaryTerms.definition: The Japanese inkstone used for grinding sumi. It has a flat grinding surface (oka) and a shallow ink-collecting well (umi) at one end.
A solid block of carbon black and animal-hide glue, formed and dried into a stick. It is ground against a wet inkstone to release ink.
The concentration of carbon particles in the ground ink, ranging from pale grey for wash work to deep black for writing. Density is controlled by water quantity and grinding time.

content.text: Why grind fresh

Bottled sumi ink is convenient but is manufactured months or years before use. The glue binder degrades over time, particles settle, and re-suspension by shaking is never complete. Freshly ground ink has an even particle distribution, superior adhesion to paper, and a lustrous black that bottled products rarely match. For serious brush work and calligraphy on good paper, grinding is worth the ten minutes it requires.
The suzuri and how it works
The
suzuri
has two zones: the flat grinding platform (oka) and the ink well (umi) at the lower end. The oka is milled with microscopic ridges that abrade the
sumi inkstick
and release carbon and glue particles. The umi collects the ground ink and keeps it from drying while you work.

The quality of the stone matters. Cheaper stones are too coarse: they grind quickly but produce particles that sit rough on paper and dry with a flat, grey tone. A fine-grained stone from Kiritani or Akama grinds slowly but produces the deep lustre associated with good calligraphy.
Water quantity

Use cold water. Warm water accelerates glue breakdown and makes the ink smell faintly of hide. A few drops placed at the top of the oka, allowed to run down toward the umi, is enough to begin. The grinding surface should be damp but not pooled.

For writing-weight ink (dense black) use fewer drops and grind longer. For wash or gradation work, add more water to the umi once the stick has released its initial concentration, or dilute directly in a separate palette well. Never add water mid-grind to the oka itself; it dilutes before enough particles have been released and you lose track of true density.
Grinding motion

Hold the inkstick vertically, not at an angle. Apply light downward pressure, no more than the weight of your hand. Move in slow, even ellipses the length of the oka, keeping contact with the stone throughout the stroke. The motion should be nearly silent. A scraping or scratching sound means too much pressure, which gouges the stone surface and shortens the life of both stick and stone.

Grind for five to ten minutes for a typical practice session. The ink in the umi will deepen from grey to a dark charcoal. Continue until the umi holds a small pool of ink that looks black when viewed straight on.
Testing ink density

The simplest density test is the fingernail drag: dip the brush, hold it at the angle you will write at, and draw a loaded stroke across scrap paper. Let it dry for 30 seconds. Pale grey or brown: too dilute, grind longer. Matte flat black: slightly too dry, the glue may resist paper and the stroke will feather. Deep, slightly shiny black: correct
ink density
for writing work.

A second test is the tip-of-brush stroke: pick up ink on just the final third of the brush hairs and make a thin stroke. It should taper cleanly to a hair-fine line without skipping or blobbing at the beginning. Skipping at the start means the density is too high and the surface tension is preventing the ink from releasing; blobbing means it is too thin.
Inkstone care
Rinse the stone under cold running water immediately after each session, using only your fingers or a soft cloth. Never use soap; it leaves a residue that repels ink at the next grinding.
Dried ink in the umi can be soaked loose with a few minutes of standing water. Do not pick at dried ink with a knife or scraper.

After rinsing, dry the stone with a soft cloth and leave it face-up in open air for at least an hour before covering. Storing a damp stone leads to mildew in the stone's pores and permanent darkening of the grinding surface.
The inkstick should also be dried thoroughly after each session. Leaving it wet in the umi causes it to crack as the outer layer expands and dries unevenly.

Over time the oka will develop a polished central channel. This is normal and improves grinding once established. Avoid grinding always in the same spot; vary the path slightly each session to wear the surface evenly.
Storage of ground ink

Ground ink deteriorates within a day or two as the glue continues to break down in water. It is best prepared fresh for each session. If you must keep it overnight, transfer it to a small sealed jar, label it with the date, and use within 24 hours. Do not pour leftover ink back over the stone to dry; it will contaminate the next grinding.
